query: effectiveness of indinavir in treating amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phenylbutyrate is a histone deacetylase inhibitor that decreases the stress response in the endoplasmic reticulum. Combined with taurursodiol (which increases the threshold for cellular apoptosis), it decreases neuronal destruction. The result is a slower decline in function. Sodium phenylbutyrate-taurursodiol is administered intrathecally and may result in myelitis, radiculitis, aseptic meningitis, and elevated intracranial pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Riluzole, Edaravone, and sodium phenylbutyrate are the only approved medications to treat ALS but provide limited survival benefits. [1] [3] Riluzole, 50mg bid, is recommended for all patients with ALS and is thought to reduce glutamate-induced excitotoxicity. Riluzole is the only medication that improves overall survival (74% vs. 58% in the placebo group at 12 months) and slows the progression of symptoms (ie, survival without tracheostomy (57% vs. 50% at 18 months). Riluzole is most effective in patients who have been symptomatic for less than 5 years, have a vital capacity (VC) >60% of the predicted value, and do not require a tracheostomy. [36] It is important to note that the elimination of riluzole will be affected by CYP1A2 inhibitors like caffeine and theophylline (decrease the rate of elimination of riluzole). Transaminase elevation occurs in approximately 50% of patients. Monitor transaminases monthly for the first three months and every three months after.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Depression is common, and treatment improves the quality of life. Amitriptyline is commonly used as it can also treat other symptoms such as insomnia, sialorrhea, and pseudobulbar affect. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are another alternative if the TCAs are not tolerated. The issue of end-of-life care and advanced directives should be broached soon after diagnosis. Treating dyspnea and anxiety is essential. In addition to NIV, relaxation techniques, psychosocial support, morphine, and benzodiazepines are helpful. Insomnia should be first addressed by treating the underlying cause (ie, dyspnea, pain, or anxiety). Tricyclic antidepressants and benzodiazepines are medication options. [20] Hospice and palliative care providers can provide many resources that are not available or as readily available in the home and can increase the likelihood of a peaceful death. [47] Ongoing trials focus on nanoparticles' role, viral vectors, monoclonal-antibody, and stem cells. [1] [3] Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning may prove to be a prodigy in management. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Edaravone is a free radical scavenger that is thought to reduce oxidative stress and slow functional progression. Edaravone provides the most benefit in patients with early ALS (ie, less than two years of symptoms, living independently, VC >80%, and scores of 2 or more in all items of ALSFRS-R) with probable or definite ALS (revised El Escorial criteria). One study showed approximately 33% slower functional decline in patients at 24 weeks follow-up. [37] Edaravone treatment is expensive ($146,000 annually). Edaravone should be used with caution in patients with asthma. It contains sodium bisulfite, which may cause asthmatic reactions in up to 5% of patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: There is no cure for ALS. [5] [7] The median survival is 3 to 5 years. Approximately 30% of patients survive 5 years and 10% ten years. Riluzole provides a survival benefit averaging 3 months, with edaravone showing a 33% reduction in the decline of the ALS Functional Rating Scale–Revised (ALSFRS-R) at 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease-modifying Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: The standard dose of Riluzole in the management of ALS is 50 mg two times daily. [58] The most common adverse effects include nausea, asthenia, and derangement in liver enzymes. [57] Pancreatitis and fulminant hepatic failure have also been reported as rare adverse effects. [59] Regular blood testing every month for the first three months, followed by three monthly for nine months and yearly, is recommended. [60] An increase of over three times the upper limit of normal in hepatic enzymes can be managed by dose reduction, followed by withdrawal followed by the re-introduction of the drug [60] . Riluzole is contraindicated in hepatic and renal impairments and in those who are breastfeeding and lactating. Further trials have been advised to test the efficacy of different dosing regimens. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Progressive Polyradiculopathy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Infective and lymphoma-related etiologies are usually subacute and progressive. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculopathy (CIDP) and diffuse infiltrative lymphocytosis (DIL) are less commonly seen. Rarely, 2 types of subacute lumbosacral polyradiculopathy syndromes are seen with HIV seroconversion, known as HIV-associated variant of Guillain Barre syndrome (GBS), a type of acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculopathy and another type with motor axonal GBS. [14] Idanivir-induced epidural lipomatosis has also been reported, which led to lumbar polyradiculopathy and resolved with the discontinuation of the drug. [15] A clinical course with milder features was also observed in a few studies, which were more benign and had lesser neurological deficits and CSF findings, unlike those seen in CMV infection, with either spontaneous recovery or quick recovery after surgical decompression. Thus, while investigating known HIV-positive patients, it is essential to exclude etiologies with a similar presentation in the general population, especially in patients with a well-controlled disease under highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART). [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: One classic presentation of ALS may not exist. Personalized therapy needs to be implemented. [3] Each patient must be evaluated for ALS mimics. Treatment at multidisciplinary ALS clinics improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease-modifying Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Edaravone has also been shown to impede disease progression in those patients with early-onset disease and rapid disease progression. [62] Its role in the management of all patients with ALS remains to be addressed. [63] The drug acts as an antioxidant, which has been shown to prevent the nitration of tyrosine residues in experimental animal models. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Indinavir (Archived) -- Adverse Effects -- Most Common Adverse Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Lipodystrophy

retrieved_docs.title: Indinavir (Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indinavir is an alpha-amino acid amide protease inhibitor used as a treatment therapy for Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infections, which can lead to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). The FDA approved indinavir in 1996 as it was among the first HIV protease inhibitors authorized in the United States. [1] Indinavir has decreased the likelihood of death due to AIDS progression.  It is no longer available in the United States or Europe as it has many adverse effects, and there are better drugs. The article remains for historical value.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with ALS will suffer from chronic respiratory failure due to weakness of the diaphragmatic and intercostal muscles. Pulmonary function tests are done every three months. As this weakness is progressive, an early, careful discussion regarding respiratory management and future options such as tracheostomy, chronic ventilatory support, and noninvasive ventilation (NIV) is warranted. The discussion should evolve with the progression of symptoms and patient readiness. NIV will be helpful when the vital capacity (VC) is less than 50%, sniff nasal inspiratory pressure (SNIP) < 40 cm H2O, pCO2 > 45mmHg, significant desaturations are noted on overnight oximetry, or when evidence of respiratory weakness is present. [20] The use of NIV prolongs survival. [5] Invasive ventilation is considered when NIV is no longer tolerated, or when the patient continues to experience hypoxia or hypercarbia despite NIV. Survival after beginning invasive ventilation is approximately 30 months. Patients should understand that they can discontinue therapy at any time during the treatment process. Immunization with the annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccines is important.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: AVP-923 (dextromethorphan-quinidine 20mg/10mg) has proven effective in patients with pseudobulbar affect. [45] Less rigorous evidence has supported the use of amitriptyline and fluvoxamine in smaller trials. Pain is treated based on its underlying cause. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications, paracetamol, intra-articular steroids, or lidocaine injections are recommended for nociceptive pain, whereas gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, and morphine are advised for neuropathic pain. Mexiletine, levetiracetam, and quinine sulfate help patients with cramps. Assistive devices such as special mattresses, pillows, and wheelchairs may help to prevent pain. Ultimately, many patients require nonopioid analgesics and anti-inflammatory medications, and when these fail, opioids become the mainstay of pain treatment. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the etiology of ALS. Identify the typical findings on the examination of a patient with ALS. Determine the interventions available for patients with ALS. Apply effective strategies to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to facilitate positive outcomes for patients with ALS. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.content: The use of antisense oligonucleotides in SOD 1 related and C9orf related ALS are subjects of research in future clinical trials. [93]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors are a class of antiretroviral agents that act by binding to HIV reverse transcriptase and inhibiting viral replication. The drugs efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin, and rilpivirine are all members of the non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same pharmacologic group helps clinicians understand their shared mechanism of action, anticipate class‑related effects, and make informed choices when selecting or switching antiretroviral therapy for patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 0 -- Community 21
retrieved_docs.content: these entities and relationships delineate a comprehensive framework of antiviral, antiretroviral, and antifungal drug classes, their structural families, mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, epidemiologic links, and relevant contraindications, thereby informing optimal clinical selection, prevention, and treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1096
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, encompass several specific agents including ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. All of these drugs share the common mechanism of inhibiting viral proteases, a broader relationship described as “inhibitory proteas.” Within this class, amprenavir is explicitly linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, underscoring its role in protease inhibition. The collective activity of these protease inhibitors as direct‑acting antivirals is central to antiviral treatment strategies, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where blocking viral protease function is essential for suppressing viral replication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, constitute a therapeutic drug class that includes ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents act by specifically inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and reducing viral replication. As key components of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, each of these drugs—ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir—shares the common mechanism of direct protease inhibition, making them integral to combination regimens aimed at achieving viral suppression and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors are a class of direct‑acting antiviral agents that target viral protease enzymes essential for the replication of certain viruses, most notably HIV. Key members of this class include fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each of which is classified as a protease inhibitor. By inhibiting the protease step in the viral life cycle, these drugs prevent the maturation of infectious viral particles and are integral components of combination antiretroviral therapy used to achieve viral suppression in patients with HIV infection. Understanding that fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir all belong to the protease inhibitor group helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, anticipate class‑related effects, and manage drug‑drug interactions within comprehensive HIV care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral medications that includes ritonavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents function by inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and serving as direct-acting antiviral therapies, particularly in the management of HIV infection. Their classification together reflects shared mechanisms of action and therapeutic use within antiviral treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating a potential therapeutic role for this vitamin in managing the neurological disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2490
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is an antiretroviral medication that functions as an inhibitory proteas, meaning it acts as a protease inhibitor that blocks the activity of viral proteases essential for HIV replication, highlighting its therapeutic role in managing HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 0 -- Community 6
retrieved_docs.content: these entities demonstrate how diverse chemical classes—DAAs, NRTIs, other nucleoside/nucleotide analogues, integrase inhibitors, NNRTIs, cyclic amines, lipopeptide antivirals, echinocandin antifungals, peptide‑derived radiopharmaceuticals, fusion‑inhibitor peptides, monoclonal antibodies, broad‑spectrum antivirals, and acid‑suppressive agents—are organized by mechanism of action, structural relationships, disease indications, and safety considerations, thereby guiding clinicians in prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of viral infections such as HCV, HIV, cytomegalovirus, orthopoxvirus, and influenza, as well as in related therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310
retrieved_docs.content: Fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir are all members of the protease inhibitor class, which constitutes a group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug targets viral protease enzymes, thereby blocking the proteolytic processing of viral polyproteins that is essential for viral replication. This mechanism makes them key components of antiretroviral therapy, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where inhibition of the HIV protease enzyme reduces viral load and slows disease progression. Recognizing that fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir share this common pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their collective role in disrupting viral life cycles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: Delavirdin, rilpivirin, and nevirapin are all members of the pharmacologic class known as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). NNRTIs constitute a group of antiretroviral agents that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a distinct non‑nucleoside site, thereby preventing viral RNA transcription and replication. By sharing this common mechanism of action, delavirdin, rilpivirin, and nevirapin are used in combination regimens to treat HIV infection, offering an alternative to nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors and contributing to the overall effectiveness of antiretroviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the antiviral agents ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir and tipranavir. Within this class, amprenavir is linked to a subcategory termed inhibitory proteas, and the broader protease‑inhibitor class is also associated with the inhibitory proteas concept. Inhibitory proteas is identified as the proteasome‑inhibiting drug ixazomib, and it encompasses the specific agent remikiren. These hierarchical and functional connections illustrate how antiviral protease inhibitors relate to proteasome‑targeting compounds, underscoring shared mechanistic themes that are important for drug classification, therapeutic decision‑making and the development of treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 91
retrieved_docs.content: classes, formulation categories, mechanisms of action, and disease associations provides clinicians and researchers with a comprehensive view of structural and therapeutic linkages that inform drug selection, combination therapy, and the development of new pharmacologic agents.
